chapter_summary: Hirsch argues that national literacy is not merely a formal skill but requires a shared network of specific information called 'cultural literacy.' He contends that American literacy levels are failing to meet the rising demands of the modern world, which threatens economic prosperity, social justice, and effective democratic communication.

claims.text: Specific educational changes are required to achieve a higher level of national literacy.
claims.source_passage: This book explains why we need to make some very specific educational changes in order to achieve a higher level of national literacy.

claims.text: Americans do not read as well as they should.
claims.source_passage: It does not document at length what has already been established, that Americans do not read as well as they should.

claims.text: Educational content must receive as much emphasis as the teaching of reading 'skills'.
claims.source_passage: It takes no position about methods of initial reading instruction beyond insisting that content must receive as much emphasis as 'skill.'

claims.text: The standard of literacy required by modern society has been rising throughout the developed world.
claims.source_passage: The standard of literacy required by modern society has been rising throughout the developed world, but American literacy rates have not risen to meet this standard.

claims.source_passage: Membership is automatic if one learns the background information and the linguistic conventions that are needed to read, write, and speak effectively.

claims.text: Literate culture should be everyone's second culture, existing beyond the narrow spheres of family, neighborhood, and region.
claims.source_passage: it is not usually one's first culture, but it should be everyone's second, existing as it does beyond the narrow spheres of family, neighborhood, and region.

claims.text: The connection between mainstream culture and national written language justifies calling mainstream culture the basic culture of the nation.
claims.source_passage: the connection between mainstream culture and the national written language justifies calling mainstream culture the basic culture of the nation.

claims.text: Traditional forms of literate culture are the most effective instruments for political and social change.
claims.source_passage: the traditional forms of literate culture are precisely the most effective instruments for political and social change.

claims.text: All political discourse at the national level must utilize the stable forms of the national language and its associated culture.
claims.source_passage: All political discourse at the national level must use the stable forms of the national language and its associated culture.

claims.text: Effective radical communication requires conservatism in the means of communication (language and spelling).
claims.source_passage: to be a conservative in the means of communication is the road to effectiveness in modern life, in whatever direction one wishes to be effective.

claims.text: Withholding traditional culture from the school curriculum in the name of progressive ideas is an unprogressive action that preserves the status quo.
claims.source_passage: To withhold traditional culture from the school curriculum, and therefore from students, in the name of progressive ideas is in fact an unprogressive action that helps preserve the political and economic status quo.

claims.text: Less privileged children fail to receive mainstream literate culture if it is not provided consistently in school.
claims.source_passage: less privileged children are denied consistent interchanges with literate persons and fail to receive this information in school.

claims.text: Providing children with traditional information does not necessarily indoctrinate them in a conservative point of view.
claims.source_passage: Providing our children with traditional information by no means indoctrinates them in a conservative point of view.

claims.text: Middle-class children acquire mainstream literate culture through daily encounters with other literate persons.
claims.source_passage: Middle-class children acquire mainstream literate culture by daily encounters with other literate persons.

claims.text: Less privileged children are denied consistent interchanges with literate persons and fail to receive literate information in school.
claims.source_passage: But less privileged children are denied consistent interchanges with literate persons and fail to receive this information in school.

claims.text: The most straightforward antidote to the deprivation of less privileged children is to make essential cultural information available inside the schools.
claims.source_passage: The most straightforward antidote to their deprivation is to make the essential information more readily available inside the schools.

claims.text: A traditional education can be subversive of the status quo rather than conservative.
claims.source_passage: Conservatives who wish to preserve traditional values will find that these are not necessarily inculcated by a traditional education, which can in fact be subversive of the status quo.

claims.text: Teaching national mainstream culture enables children to understand and predict the typical attitudes of other Americans without forcing them to accept those values.
claims.source_passage: Although teaching children national mainstream culture doesn't mean forcing them to accept its values uncritically, it does enable them to understand those values in order to predict the typical attitudes of other Americans.

claims.text: Values affirmed in traditional literate culture can serve a whole spectrum of different value attitudes.
claims.source_passage: For us no less than for Wilde, the values affirmed in traditional literate culture can serve a whole spectrum of value attitudes.

claims.text: Schools have a fundamental duty to teach shared content regardless of the complexities of teaching values.
claims.source_passage: But the complex problem of how to teach values in American schools mustn't distract attention from our fundamental duty to teach shared content.

claims.text: The failure of schools to create a literate society is frequently excused by the claim that schools are asked to do too much.
claims.source_passage: The failure of our schools to create a literate society is sometimes excused on the grounds that the schools have been asked to do too much.

claims.text: Schools cannot teach everything and must establish educational priorities.
claims.source_passage: In some of our national moods we would like the schools to teach everything, but they cannot. There is a pressing need for clarity about our educational priorities.

claims.text: The concept of cultural literacy prioritizes national information over local information for the sake of wider communication.
claims.source_passage: The concept of cultural literacy helps us to make such decisions because it places a higher value on national than on local information. We want to make our children competent to communicate with Americans throughout the land.

claims.text: To become literate, it is more important for an American child to know about Abraham Lincoln than Jeb Stuart.
claims.source_passage: Therefore, if Virginians did have to decide between Stuart and Lincoln they ought to favor the man from Illinois over the one from Virginia.

claims.text: Educational priority for specific figures like Lincoln over Stuart is based on communicative utility, not inherent merit.
claims.source_passage: The priority has nothing to do with inherent merit, only with the accidents of culture.

claims.text: Educational policy always involves choices between degrees of worthiness.
claims.source_passage: Educational policy always involves choices between degrees of worthiness.

claims.text: The concept of cultural literacy places a higher value on national than on local information.
claims.source_passage: The concept of cultural literacy helps us to make such decisions because it places a higher value on national than on local information.

claims.text: The educational priority of cultural figures is based on the accidents of culture rather than inherent moral merit.
claims.source_passage: The priority has nothing to do with inherent merit, only with the accidents of culture.

claims.text: Benedict Arnold should be given educational priority over Jeb Stuart because Arnold is a standard reference in the national language.
claims.source_passage: Stuart certainly had more merit than Benedict Arnold did, but Arnold also should be given educational priority over Stuart. Why? Because Benedict Arnold is as much a part of our national language as is, say, Judas.

claims.text: Cultural literacy functions as a shared national vocabulary that allows for communication throughout a society.
claims.source_passage: To describe Benedict Arnold and Abraham Lincoln as belonging to the national language discloses another way of conceiving cultural literacy — as a vocabulary that we are able to use throughout the land because we share associations with others in our society.

claims.text: A universally shared national vocabulary is analogous to a universal currency.
claims.source_passage: A universally shared national vocabulary is analogous to a universal currency like the dollar.

claims.text: Society has a duty to determine and disclose the contents of cultural literacy to those who lack it.
claims.source_passage: Once we become aware of the inherent connection between literacy and cultural literacy, we have a duty to those who lack cultural literacy to determine and disclose its contents.

claims.text: Writers must make assumptions about the body of information their readers know in order to communicate.
claims.source_passage: Writers must make assumptions about the body of information their readers know.

claims.text: The total quantity of commonly shared information needed for literacy is telegraphic, vague, and limited in extent.
claims.source_passage: the total quantity of commonly shared information that the schools need to impart is less daunting than one might think, for the crucial background knowledge possessed by literate people is, as I have pointed out, telegraphic, vague, and limited in extent.

claims.text: Instruction in literate national culture should begin in preschool.
claims.source_passage: Preschool is not too early for starting earnest instruction in literate national culture.

claims.text: By tenth grade, it is usually too late to begin imparting the background knowledge required for cultural literacy.
claims.source_passage: Tenth grade usually is too late.

claims.text: Disadvantaged children often have acceptable decoding skills but cannot understand a text as a whole because they lack background knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Although disadvantaged children often show an acceptable ability to decode and pronounce individual words, they are frequently unable to gain an integrated sense of a piece as a whole. They miss central implications and associations because they don't possess the background knowledge necessary to put the text in context.

claims.text: There is no significant difference in reading performance between social classes in kindergarten and first grade.
claims.source_passage: Yet if you observe a kindergarten or first-grade class in which pupils have the same diversity of family background, you will not find a similar spread in the reading performances of pupils from different social classes.

claims.text: The United States performs well internationally in mechanical reading skills but poorly in interpretive reading skills.
claims.source_passage: Before grade three, when reading skills are more mechanical than interpretive, the United States stands in the top group of countries. Later, when reading requires an understanding of more complex content, our comparative ranking drops.

claims.text: American schools teach elementary decoding skills well but perform poorly compared to other countries in teaching the background knowledge required for mature reading.
claims.source_passage: while American schools may do well in teaching elementary decoding skills, they do less well than schools of some other countries in teaching the background knowledge that pupils must possess to succeed at mature reading tasks.

claims.text: Teaching texts with cultural content instead of developmental texts in early grades could overcome the knowledge deficit of disadvantaged children.
claims.source_passage: If in the early grades our children were taught texts with cultural content rather than 'developmental' texts that develop abstract skills, much of the specific knowledge deficit of disadvantaged children could be overcome.

claims.text: Reading and writing are cumulative skills; background knowledge does not develop automatically.
claims.source_passage: Background knowledge does not take care of itself. Reading and writing are cumulative skills

claims.text: A critical difference in reading performance between disadvantaged and advantaged fifth-graders is the difference in their cultural knowledge.
claims.source_passage: For it is clear that one critical difference in the reading performances of disadvantaged fifth-graders as compared with advantaged pupils is the difference in their cultural knowledge.

claims.text: Background knowledge does not develop automatically; it must be explicitly taught.
claims.source_passage: Background knowledge does not take care of itself.

claims.text: Reading and writing are cumulative skills where existing knowledge facilitates the acquisition of more knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Reading and writing are cumulative skills; the more we read the more necessary knowledge we gain for further reading.

claims.text: Students who lack initial cultural knowledge begin to be left behind permanently around grade four.
claims.source_passage: Around grade four, those who lack the initial knowledge required for significant reading begin to be left behind permanently.

claims.text: Teaching cultural information in early grades enhances the motivation and self-esteem of disadvantaged children by removing a cause of failure.
claims.source_passage: By removing one of the causes of failure, it would especially enhance the motivation, self-esteem, and performance of disadvantaged children.

claims.text: Educational reforms in cultural literacy must begin in the earliest grades to be effective.
claims.source_passage: Really effective reforms in the teaching of cultural literacy must therefore begin with the earliest grades.

claims.text: Teaching literate background information to young children creates a multiplier effect on later learning.
claims.source_passage: Every improvement made in teaching very young children literate background information will have a multiplier effect on later learning

claims.text: Young children enjoy the process of absorbing formulaic knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Young children enjoy absorbing formulaic knowledge.

claims.text: Critical thinking and basic skills alone cannot enable children to generate historically accidental names and concepts.
claims.source_passage: Critical thinking and basic skills, two areas of current focus in education, do not enable children to create out of their own imaginations the essential names and concepts that have arisen by historical accident.

claims.text: Facts are essential components of basic skills.
claims.source_passage: Facts are essential components of the basic skills that a child entering a culture must have.

claims.text: The content of cultural literacy changes over time due to history, subnational cultures, and scientific advancement.
claims.source_passage: It's obvious that the content of cultural literacy changes over the years. ... Other changes come through the contributions of various subnational cultures. ... Other subnational cultures, including those of science and technology, also cause changes in the mainstream culture.

claims.text: Stability, rather than change, is the primary characteristic of cultural literacy.
claims.source_passage: But stability, not change, is the chief characteristic of cultural literacy.

claims.text: Persistent and stable elements of national vocabulary should form the core of school curricula.
claims.source_passage: These stable elements of the national vocabulary are at the core of cultural literacy, and for that reason are the most important contents of schooling.

claims.text: American writers and speakers assume that their audience possesses a general knowledge of specific historical figures as a starting point for communication.
claims.source_passage: American readers are assumed to have a general knowledge of the following people... it enables writers and speakers to assume a starting point from which they can treat in detail what they wish to focus on.

claims.text: The persistent and stable elements of literate culture belong at the core of the educational curriculum.
claims.source_passage: The persistent, stable elements belong at the educational core.

claims.text: Critical thinking skills courses are unable to generate the specific factual knowledge, such as geographical names, required for literacy.
claims.source_passage: Here is another alphabetical list that no course in critical thinking skills, however masterful, could ever generate: Antarctic Ocean, Arctic Ocean, Atlantic Ocean...

claims.text: Children must acquire specific names and geographical facts because literate people mention them without explanation.
claims.source_passage: Because literate people mention such names in passing, usually without explanation, children should acquire them as part of their intellectual equipment.

claims.text: Literacy requires an understanding of shared literary and mythic heritage that is frequently alluded to without explanation.
claims.source_passage: Children also need to understand elements of our literary and mythic heritage that are often alluded to without explanation

claims.text: The modern educational distaste for memorization is unrealistic and more ideological than practical.
claims.source_passage: Our current distaste for memorization is more pious than realistic.

claims.text: Children have an instinctive urge to learn specific tribal traditions and catalogues of information at an early age.
claims.source_passage: At an early age when their memories are most retentive, children have an almost instinctive urge to learn specific tribal traditions. At that age they seem to be fascinated by catalogues of information

claims.text: Children seek to master specific cultural materials to authenticate their membership in adult society.
claims.source_passage: and are eager to master the materials that authenticate their membership in adult society.

claims.text: Global human tradition suggests that basic acculturation into a society should be completed by age thirteen.
claims.source_passage: The weight of human tradition across many cultures supports the view that basic acculturation should largely be completed by age thirteen.

claims.text: All cultures documented in standard anthropological records utilize early memorization to transmit their traditions.
claims.source_passage: According to the anthropological record, all cultures whose educational methods have been reported in the Human Relations Area Files (a standard source for anthropological data) have used early memorization to carry on their traditions.

claims.text: The educational theory of natural development encourages independence of mind but possesses significant drawbacks.
claims.source_passage: But the theory also has its drawbacks, one of which is that a child is not in fact like an acorn. Left to itself, a child will not grow into a thriving creature; Tarzan is pure fantasy.

claims.text: The theory that children develop naturally like acorns is a false analogy because children require specific cultural learning to thrive.
claims.source_passage: But the theory also has its drawbacks, one of which is that a child is not in fact like an acorn. Left to itself, a child will not grow into a thriving creature; Tarzan is pure fantasy.

claims.text: The opportunity to acquire cultural literacy is usually lost permanently if it is not gained in the early grades.
claims.source_passage: The opportunity of acquiring cultural literacy, once lost in the early grades is usually lost for good.

claims.text: Children of parents who lack knowledge of the national literate culture are the most likely to permanently lose the opportunity for cultural literacy.
claims.source_passage: That is most likely to be true for children of parents who were not themselves taught the literate national culture.

claims.text: A child is fundamentally unlike an acorn; they will not grow into a thriving creature if left to develop in isolation.
claims.source_passage: But the theory also has its drawbacks, one of which is that a child is not in fact like an acorn. Left to itself, a child will not grow into a thriving creature

claims.text: To thrive, a child must learn the traditions of the specific human society and culture into which they are born.
claims.source_passage: To thrive, a child needs to learn the traditions of the particular human society and culture it is born into.

claims.text: American children require traditional information at a very early age.
claims.source_passage: Like children everywhere, American children need traditional information at a very early age.

claims.text: Shared materials of literate culture, such as historical and geographical knowledge, have become more important in the technological age.
claims.source_passage: In the technological age, Washington and the cherry tree, Scrooge and Christmas, the fights historical, the oceans geographical, the "beings animalculus," and all the other shared materials of literate culture have become more, not less, important.

claims.text: As civilization becomes more specialized and technical, it becomes increasingly difficult for nonspecialists to participate in democratic decision-making.
claims.source_passage: The more specialized and technical our civilization becomes, the harder it is for nonspecialists to participate in the decisions that deeply affect their lives.

claims.text: A failure to achieve a literate society will result in a communication breakdown between technical specialists and the general public.
claims.source_passage: If we do not achieve a literate society, the technicians, with their arcane specialties, will not be able to communicate with us nor we with them.

claims.text: Communication barriers between specialists and the public contradict the basic principles of democracy.
claims.source_passage: That would contradict the basic principles of democracy and must not be allowed to happen.

claims.text: The remedy for growing specialization is the reinvigoration of the unspecialized domain of literate discourse.
claims.source_passage: The antidote to growing specialization is to reinvigigorate the unspecialized domain of literate discourse, where all can meet on common ground.

claims.text: A just and prosperous society is only possible if schools ensure all children possess the shared background knowledge required for effective communication.
claims.source_passage: We will be able to achieve a just and prosperous society only when our schools ensure that everyone commands enough shared background knowledge to be able to communicate effectively with everyone else.

claims.text: The primary goal of the book is to shift focus away from 'the great debate' regarding methods of reading instruction and toward the problem of educational content.
claims.source_passage: In fact, one of its major purposes is to break away entirely from what Jeanne S. Chall has called 'the great debate' about methods of reading instruction. It focuses on what I conceive to be the great hidden problem in American education

claims.text: The moral of the Tower of Babel story is that complex human undertakings fail when communication systems fail.
claims.source_passage: Where communications fail, so do the undertakings. (That is the moral of the story of the Tower of Babel.)

claims.text: Effective communication between strangers requires a subconscious estimation of how much information can be taken for granted in the other person.
claims.source_passage: Passers-by were intuitively aware that communication between strangers requires an estimate of how much relevant information can be taken for granted in the other person.

claims.text: Although nationalism may have regrettable global political effects, mastering national culture is a prerequisite for mastering a nation's standard language.
claims.source_passage: Although nationalism may be regrettable in some of its worldwide political effects, a mastery of national culture is essential to mastery of the standard language in every modern nation.

claims.text: Educational reformers should focus undeviatingly on school curriculum rather than external factors like family structure, social class, or television programming.
claims.source_passage: But we should direct our attention undeviatingly toward what the schools teach rather than toward family structure, social class, or TV programming.

claims.text: The effectiveness of radical political discourse, such as that of The Black Panther, depends on the writers' rigorous traditional education in national history and literature.
claims.source_passage: The writers for The Black Panther had clearly received a rigorous traditional education in American history, in the Declaration of Independence, the Pledge of Allegiance to the Flag, the Gettysburg Address, and the Bible

claims.text: Oscar Wilde's satire of the George Washington cherry-tree myth is more effective for American audiences than British ones due to specific shared cultural associations.
claims.source_passage: Far from accepting the cherry-tree tale or its implications, Oscar Wilde in 'The Decay of Lying' used it ironically, in a way that is probably funnier to Americans than to the British audience he was addressing.

claims.text: Cultural literacy serves as the 'oxygen of social intercourse,' only becoming noticeable when it is absent or deficient.
claims.source_passage: We ignore the air we breathe until it is thin or foul. Cultural literacy is the oxygen of social intercourse. Only when we run into cultural illiteracy are we shocked into recognizing the importance of the information that we had unconsciously assumed.

claims.text: The ability to predict the typical attitudes of other citizens is a primary benefit of being taught national mainstream culture.
claims.source_passage: it does enable them to understand those values in order to predict the typical attitudes of other Americans.

claims.text: Japan's industrial efficiency is specifically attributed to its universally high level of literacy.
claims.source_passage: Much of Japan's industrial efficiency has been credited to its almost universally high level of literacy.

evidence.description: Empirical study showing rising standards of literacy across fifteen countries.
evidence.source_reference: R. L. Thorndike, Reading Comprehension Education in Fifteen Countries: An Empirical Study (1973)
evidence.source_passage: For rising standards of literacy, see R. L. Thorndike, Reading Comprehension Education in Fifteen Countries: An Empirical Study

evidence.description: Analysis crediting Japan's industrial efficiency to its high level of literacy.
evidence.source_reference: Thomas P. Rohlen, 'Japanese Education: If They Can Do It, Should We?' (1985-86)
evidence.source_passage: Much of Japan's industrial efficiency has been credited to its almost universally high level of literacy.

evidence.description: Report documenting American literacy rates and the state of reading instruction.
evidence.source_reference: Jeanne Chall, 'Afterword,' in Becoming a Nation of Readers (1985)
evidence.source_passage: For American literacy rates see Jeanne Chall, 'Afterword,' in R. C. Anderson et al., Becoming a Nation of Readers

evidence.description: Linguistic analysis of the stabilizing effects mass literacy has on oral speech.
evidence.source_reference: Henry Bradley, The Making of English (1968); Otto Jespersen, Mankind, Nation, and Individual (1964)
evidence.source_passage: The two classical discussions of the stabilizing effects of mass literacy on oral speech are Henry Bradley, The Making of English... and Otto Jespersen, Mankind, Nation, and Individual

evidence.description: The Harvard Square experiment by Krauss and Glucksberg showing that speakers provide more detailed and rudimentary instructions to perceived 'out-of-towners' compared to 'natives.'
evidence.source_reference: Krauss and Glucksberg, 'Social and Nonsocial Speech,' Scientific American 236 (1977)
evidence.source_passage: Passers-by, thinking they are addressing a fellow Bostonian, don't even break their stride when they give their replies... [as a tourist] people's answers are much longer and more rudimentary.

evidence.description: National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP) data from 1970-1980 showing a decline in the reading performance of 17-year-olds.
evidence.source_reference: NAEP, Three National Assessments of Reading (1981)
evidence.source_passage: shows that between 1970 and 1980 seventeen-year-olds declined in their ability to understand written materials

evidence.description: SAT verbal score trends over fifteen years showing a dramatic decline from a low base, even among high-scoring students.
evidence.source_reference: College Board, College-Bound Seniors (1984)
evidence.source_passage: verbal SAT scores have declined dramatically in the past fifteen years... performance on the verbal SAT has been slipping steadily at the top.

evidence.description: SAT data showing a 56% drop in students scoring above 600 and a 73% drop in students scoring above 650 between 1972 and 1984.
evidence.source_reference: The College Board, College-Bound Seniors (1984)
evidence.source_passage: out of a constant pool of about a million test takers each year, 56 percent more students scored above 600 in 1972 than did so in 1984. More startling yet, the percentage drop was even greater for those scoring above 650 — 73 percent.

evidence.description: A meeting and subsequent study grant by major US corporations (CBS, Exxon) to the American Academy of Arts and Sciences regarding middle-management communication failures.
evidence.source_reference: Hirsch (referencing CBS/Exxon executives)
evidence.source_passage: Recently, top executives of some large U.S. companies, including CBS and Exxon, met to discuss the fact that their younger middle-level executives could no longer communicate their ideas effectively in speech or writing.

evidence.description: National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP) surveys cited as solid evidence of literacy decline.
evidence.source_reference: NAEP, Three National Assessments of Reading (1981)
evidence.source_passage: The figures from NAEP surveys and the scores on the verbal SAT are solid evidence that literacy has been declining in this country

evidence.description: Anecdotes from John Hirsch's Latin classroom where students confused 'Latin America' with the Latin language and 'The Alamo' with Homer's epics.
evidence.source_reference: Personal communication, John Hirsch
evidence.source_passage: One girl raised her hand to challenge my son's claim. 'What do they speak in Latin America?' she demanded... Another day my son asked his Latin class if they knew the name of an epic poem by Homer. One pupil shot up his hand and eagerly said, 'The Alamo!'

evidence.description: Benjamin Stein's focus group interviews showing teenagers could not identify dates for WWII, WWI, or the Civil War, nor locate Chicago or identify Thomas Jefferson.
evidence.source_reference: Benjamin J. Stein, 'The Cheerful Ignorance of the Young in L.A.,' Washington Post (1983)
evidence.source_passage: I have not yet found one single student in Los Angeles, in either college or high school, who could tell me the years when World War II was fought... Only two could even approximately identify Thomas Jefferson.

evidence.description: Anecdotes of geography and history ignorance among UCLA and USC juniors collected by Benjamin Stein.
evidence.source_reference: Stein, Benjamin J., 'The Cheerful Ignorance of the Young in L.A.,' Washington Post, 1983
evidence.source_passage: the junior at the University of California at Los Angeles who thought that Toronto must be in Italy. My second-favorite geography lesson is the junior at USC... who thought that Washington, D.C. was in Washington State.

evidence.description: NAEP data showing significant drops in civics knowledge among 13-year-olds.
evidence.source_reference: National Assessment of Educational Progress, 1978
evidence.source_passage: In 1978 NAEP issued a report which analyzed a large quantity of data showing that our children's knowledge of American civics had dropped significantly between 1969 and 1976.

evidence.description: Preliminary field test results from the Foundations of Literacy project measuring history and literature knowledge of 17-year-olds.
evidence.source_reference: Foundations of Literacy project / NAEP study, 1985-1987
evidence.source_passage: Two thirds of our seventeen-year-olds do not know that the Civil War occurred between 1850 and 1900. Three quarters do not know what reconstruction means.

evidence.description: An experiment in Richmond, Virginia where 17- and 18-year-olds could not identify Grant and Lee.
evidence.source_reference: Hirsch, 1970s experiment
evidence.source_passage: in one experiment conducted in Richmond, Virginia, our seventeen- and eighteen-year-old subjects did not know who Grant and Lee were.

evidence.description: The second part of the Cambridge experiment showing that strangers lacking shared context use uncertain and rudimentary explanations.
evidence.source_reference: Krauss and Glucksberg, 'Social and Nonsocial Speech,' 1977
evidence.source_passage: If young people meet as strangers, their communications resemble the uncertain, rudimentary explanations recorded in the second part of the Cambridge experiment.

evidence.description: Anecdotal evidence of the efficiency of quoting Shakespeare in business correspondence.
evidence.source_reference: Hirsch's personal accounts of his father
evidence.source_passage: My father used to write business letters that alluded to Shakespeare. These allusions were effective for conveying complex messages to his associates

evidence.description: Testimony from Orlando Patterson regarding the necessity of cultural literacy for social mobility.
evidence.source_reference: Orlando Patterson, 'Language, Ethnicity, and Change,' 1980
evidence.source_passage: He seconded my argument that shared information is a necessary background to true literacy. Then he extended and deepened the ideas I had presented.

evidence.description: The 'I Have a Dream' speech as a renewal of traditional American social goals of equality and merit.
evidence.source_reference: Martin Luther King, Jr., 'I Have a Dream' speech

evidence.description: Thomas Jefferson's political philosophy regarding the necessity of newspapers and a literate citizenry for democracy.
evidence.source_reference: Letter to Colonel Edward Carrington, January 16, 1787
evidence.source_passage: Were it left to me to decide whether we should have a government without newspapers, or newspapers without a government, I should not hesitate a moment to prefer the latter.

evidence.description: The lack of trust and participation in the political system by illiterate or semiliterate Americans.
evidence.source_reference: General observation of political behavior/voting turnouts

evidence.description: A letter from Thomas Jefferson expressing a preference for newspapers without a government over a government without newspapers, emphasizing the necessity of citizens being capable of reading them.
evidence.source_reference: Jefferson, Thomas. Letter to Colonel Edward Carrington, January 16, 1787
evidence.source_passage: Were it left to me to decide whether we should have a government without newspapers, or newspapers without a government, I should not hesitate a moment to prefer the latter. But I should mean that every man should receive those papers and be capable of reading them.

evidence.description: An excerpt from the Washington Post discussing a federal appeals panel's ruling on farm foreclosures, used to show the breadth of assumed knowledge (USDA, Missouri, legal systems) required for basic comprehension.
evidence.source_reference: Washington Post, December 29, 1983
evidence.source_passage: A federal appeals panel today upheld an order barring foreclosure on a Missouri farm, saying that U.S. Agriculture Secretary John R. Block has reneged on his responsibilities to some debt ridden farmers.

evidence.description: The success and humor of the book '1066 and All That' demonstrates that British citizens share a body of historical knowledge that remains functional even when the details are forgotten or vague.
evidence.source_reference: W. C. Sellar and R. J. Yeatman, 1066 and All That (1947)
evidence.source_passage: The book was hilarious to literate Britons as a satire of their own vague and confused memories. But even if their schoolchild knowledge had become vague with the passage of time, it was still functional

evidence.description: An article about 'beanballs' in baseball which provides a definition for readers who might not know the specific term but assumes they know the general context of baseball.
evidence.source_reference: Melvin Durslag, 'To Ban the Beanball,' TV Guide, June 8-14, 1985
evidence.source_passage: Described variously as the knockdown pitch, the beanball, the duster and the purpose pitch — the Pentagon would call it the peacekeeper — this delightful stratagem has graced the scene for most of the 109 years the major leagues have existed.

evidence.description: Philosophical analysis of the meaning of words like 'gold' and 'elm' and how they function in communication despite speaker ignorance of technical details.
evidence.source_reference: H. Putnam, 'The Meaning of Meaning,' in Philosophical Papers, Volume 2: Mind, Language and Reality (1975)
evidence.source_passage: Suppose you are like me and cannot tell an elm from a beech tree. ... there is a division of linguistic labor.

evidence.description: Hilary Putnam's example regarding the shared knowledge of tigers and leopards versus the specific differences between elm and beech trees.
evidence.source_reference: H. Putnam, 'The Meaning of Meaning' (1975)

evidence.description: The song 'Waltzing Matilda' contains vocabulary (swagman, billy-bong, kulibar) understood by Australians but opaque to Americans, despite a shared language.
evidence.source_reference: Common cultural artifact

evidence.description: An experiment by Richard C. Anderson comparing Indian and American readers' comprehension of letters describing weddings in their respective cultures.
evidence.source_reference: M. S. Steffensen, C. Joag-Des, and R. C. Anderson, 'A Cross-Cultural Perspective on Reading Comprehension' (1979)
evidence.source_passage: The Indians performed well in reading about the Indian wedding but poorly in reading about the American one, and the Americans did the opposite.

evidence.description: Research finding that the school curriculum is the most important controllable influence on what children know.
evidence.source_reference: Walberg and Shanahan, 'High School Effects on Individual Students' (1983)
evidence.source_passage: Researchers who have studied the factors influencing educational outcomes have found that the school curriculum is the most important controllable influence on what our children know and don't know about our literate culture.

evidence.description: Data showing a slight positive relationship between TV watching (up to 10 hours) and school achievement.
evidence.source_reference: Anderson et al., Becoming a Nation of Readers (1985)
evidence.source_passage: Up to about ten hours a week, there is actually a slight positive relationship between the amount of time children spend watching TV and their school achievement

evidence.description: Report on first-hand study of high schools showing curriculum fragmentation.
evidence.source_reference: Arthur G. Powell, Eleanor Farrar, and David K. Cohen, The Shopping Mall High School (1985)

evidence.description: Expert testimony on school responsibility for student time usage and homework.
evidence.source_reference: Herbert Walberg

evidence.description: The Black Panther newspaper demonstrated high conservatism in language and cultural assumptions to communicate radical sentiment.
The Black Panther Party platform (1972) repeated the first five hundred words of the Declaration of Independence verbatim.
A government report cited by The Black Panther stated one million youths aged 12-17 were illiterate.
evidence.source_reference: The Black Panther (May 18, 1974)

evidence.description: Hirsch's personal experience turning against conservative family views after receiving a traditional education that enabled him to read Myrdal.
The writers for The Black Panther quoting the Declaration of Independence to communicate their message.
Oscar Wilde's ironic use of the George Washington cherry tree myth in 'The Decay of Lying'.
evidence.source_reference: Oscar Wilde, 'The Decay of Lying' (1889)

evidence.description: List of non-academic subjects schools are asked to teach (CPR, driving, consumerism, etc.) that distract from core acculturation.
The hypothetical trade-off between teaching Jeb Stuart (local Virginia figure) and Abraham Lincoln (national figure).
Comparison of fifth-graders' ability to summarize texts based on their cultural background.
evidence.source_reference: Jeanne S. Chall, 'Afterword,' in Becoming a Nation of Readers (1985)
evidence.source_passage: There are predictable differences between the summaries given by children with culturally adequate backgrounds and those given by children without.

evidence.description: Study showing disadvantaged first-graders perform as well as middle-class ones in sounding out letters.
evidence.source_reference: J. S. Chall, C. Snow, et al., Families and Literacy (1982)
evidence.source_passage: Disadvantaged first-graders do as well as middle class ones in sounding out letters and simple words.

evidence.description: International comparison of reading attainment in fifteen countries showing US ranking drops as reading becomes interpretive.
evidence.source_reference: R. L. Thorndike, Reading Comprehension Education in Fifteen Countries (1973)
evidence.source_passage: the United States stands in the top group of countries. Later, when reading requires an understanding of more complex content, our comparative ranking drops.

evidence.description: Comparison of decoding skills versus background knowledge instruction between US schools and other countries.
Observation of reading performance gaps between advantaged and disadvantaged 5th graders linked to cultural knowledge.
evidence.source_reference: Chall, Snow, et al., Families and Literacy (1982)

evidence.description: The 'Fourth Grade Slump' where students without background knowledge fall behind permanently.
evidence.source_reference: Chall, Stages of Reading Development (1983)

evidence.description: Historical shifts in vocabulary: 'Brown decision' (new), 'Harold Ickes' (faded), 'DNA' (new), 'Pizza' (added).
Stable cultural referents: George Washington, Gettysburg Address, Hamlet, Declaration of Independence.
List of 20 historical figures (Adams to Hawthorne) assumed to be known by American readers.
Lists of oceans and mountain ranges used as passing references in literate discourse.
Children's ability to memorize complex sports statistics (football, baseball, basketball) without formal instruction.
Data from more than 200 entries in the Human Relations Area Files (1899-1956).
evidence.source_reference: Human Relations Area Files

evidence.description: Specific cross-cultural examples of memorization in education: Korea (Kyung and Su), Tibet (scriptures), Chile (Araucanian songs), and Africa (!Kung bushmen).
The cultural trope of Tarzan is cited as a 'pure fantasy' to illustrate that humans do not thrive without cultural transmission.
Specific writers (White, Galbraith, Thomas, Medawar, Feynman) are cited as proof that experts can communicate complex ideas to a broad literate audience.

concepts.author_definition: The network of information that all competent readers possess; background information stored in the mind that enables comprehension of implications and unstated contexts in texts like newspapers.
concepts.source_passage: What she calls world knowledge I call cultural literacy, namely, the network of information that all competent readers possess.

concepts.author_definition: A standardized form of language (like standard written English) that is fixed in dictionaries and grammars, serving as a stable instrument for communication over time and space.
concepts.common_alternative: Regional dialects or non-standard oral forms.
concepts.source_passage: the essence of literacy is not simply reading and writing but also the effective use of the standard literate language.

concepts.author_definition: Jeanne Chall's term for the background information essential to the development of reading and writing skills; equated by the author with cultural literacy.

The stable, fixed form of the English language found in dictionaries and grammars that serves as the national instrument for complex communication and has standardized the oral speech of educated citizens.
concepts.common_alternative: Sometimes viewed as just one of many equally valid dialects, or strictly limited to formal writing rather than affecting speech.
concepts.source_passage: standard spoken English is based upon forms that have been fixed in dictionaries and grammars and are adhered to in books, magazines, and newspapers.

concepts.author_definition: The ability to give and receive complex information through the standard language across time and space, requiring significant background knowledge.
concepts.common_alternative: The mechanical ability to decode words on a page.
concepts.source_passage: The chief function of literacy is to make us masters of this standard instrument of knowledge and communication, thereby enabling us to give and receive complex information orally and in writing over time and space.

concepts.author_definition: The body of information that a speaker or writer can take for granted as being known by their audience, allowing for effective communication.
concepts.common_alternative: Often treated as mere 'trivia' or disconnected facts in progressive educational theory.
concepts.source_passage: the amount of shared knowledge that we have been able to take for granted in communicating with our fellow citizens has also been declining.

concepts.author_definition: Specific historical and cultural information shared across different generations of a society, traditionally assumed by writers and necessary for national continuity.
Knowledge that is shared within a specific peer group or generation but is narrowly confined and lacks the permanence or breadth required for broader cultural literacy.
The conceptualization of reading and writing as general, formal processes that can be mastered and applied independently of specific background knowledge.
concepts.common_alternative: The prevailing view in many education schools that literacy is a transferable set of cognitive tools.

concepts.author_definition: The system of shared symbols and information required for participation in modern industrial society, decoupled from ethnic white-Anglo-Saxon-Protestant identity.
concepts.common_alternative: Viewed by some as an ethnocentric or exclusionary white culture.
concepts.source_passage: mainstream culture, which no longer has much to do with white Anglo-Saxon Protestants, but with the imperatives of industrial civilization.

concepts.author_definition: A model where the national culture is constantly changing and open, with each group participating, contributing, and being transformed by the exchange.
concepts.common_alternative: A model of domination where one group (e.g., WASPs) imposes its static culture on others.
concepts.source_passage: the accurate metaphor or model for this wider literacy is not domination, but dialectic

concepts.author_definition: Narrow vocational or job-related reading skills that do not transfer to general public discourse or new technological developments.
A person who possesses the shared background knowledge and symbols known by other literate members of the culture.
concepts.common_alternative: A generic reader with basic reading skills but not necessarily specific cultural knowledge.
concepts.source_passage: Books and newspapers assume a "common reader," that is, a person who knows the things known by other literate persons in the culture.

concepts.author_definition: The explicit words of a text which merely point toward the underlying meaning that must be completed by the reader's background knowledge.
The imprecise, non-detailed nature of the background information required for cultural literacy; the ability to recognize references without necessarily knowing every precise detail.
A societal arrangement where knowledge is distributed, allowing individuals to use words correctly and communicate effectively without possessing expert knowledge of the term's referent.
The minimal set of vague or superficial traits associated with a word that a speaker must know to be considered competent in using that word within a culture.
The crucial social knowledge of what other members of a linguistic community are likely to know or not know, allowing a speaker to judge what needs explanation.
The primary duty of schools to ensure children master the shared symbols and information of their national literate culture.
Educational theories that prioritize the development of general skills over the transmission of specific, shared knowledge.
concepts.source_passage: Our elementary schools are... dominated by the content-neutral ideas of Rousseau and Dewey

concepts.author_definition: A secondary school characterized by an 'astonishing variety' of course offerings, including academic, sports, hobbies, and 'services' curricula, where all are deemed equally valid for credit.
The theory that any suitable content will inculcate reading, writing, and thinking skills, allowing administrators to remain neutral about specific knowledge.
Literate culture that exists beyond narrow spheres of family, neighborhood, or region, serving as a common currency for a nation.
Mainstream culture as defined by its connection to the national written language.
The process of teaching knowledge that allows a citizen to communicate with anyone across the entire country, prioritized over local knowledge.
The necessary selection of specific content to teach given that schools have limited time and cannot teach everything.
A shared vocabulary of associations and cultural references (like Abraham Lincoln or Benedict Arnold) that enables communication across a diverse nation.
Texts used in early schooling that focus on developing abstract reading skills rather than conveying specific cultural content.
concepts.common_alternative: Standardized literacy curriculum focused on process over content.

concepts.author_definition: A perception held by the disadvantaged that the hidden requirements of cultural literacy are a deliberate attempt to exclude them from social success.
The process by which early acquisition of background information exponentially increases the efficiency and motivation for later learning.
Simple, often arbitrary facts, traditions, and names that children can easily absorb and which form the basis of communication.
The persistent, stable elements of national vocabulary (historical figures, foundational documents) that should be the primary focus of school content.
Terms and names that ebb and flow with recent history or subnational trends; important at a given time but not as foundational as core elements.
The theory, derived from Rousseau and Dewey, that children develop intellectually through natural growth rather than the accumulation of specific cultural information.
The process of initiating children into the shared traditions and knowledge of a tribe or society, typically completed by age thirteen.
The common ground of communication where specialists and the general public can interact because they share a common vocabulary of historical, literary, and geographical references.
Jeanne S. Chall's term for the ongoing pedagogical conflict regarding methods of initial reading instruction (e.g., phonics vs. whole language).
Hirsch's term for the neglect of specific cultural content in American education, which he identifies as the root cause of the literacy crisis.
The background information and implicit associations that alone provide meaning to a written or spoken text.
The national literate language, used to give and receive complex information over time and space.
A metaphor for literate culture as a shared medium of social and economic exchange in a democracy.
The minimum level of vague, generalized information required to successfully use and understand a word within a linguistic community.

cases.name: Japan
cases.description: A society whose industrial efficiency and economic success are attributed to near-universal high levels of literacy.
key_passages: Much of Japan's industrial efficiency has been credited to its almost universally high level of literacy.

cases.name: The Tower of Babel
cases.description: A biblical story used as a metaphor for the failure of complex human undertakings when communication and a shared language fail.
key_passages: Where communications fail, so do the undertakings. (That is the moral of the story of the Tower of Babel.)

cases.name: The Harvard Square Experiment
cases.description: A researcher in Cambridge, MA, tested how passers-by gave directions based on the perceived background knowledge of the asker. When the asker appeared to be a 'native' (carrying a local paper), replies were brief and relied on shared knowledge. When the asker presented as a 'tourist,' replies were significantly longer and more basic, explaining tokens, subway signs, and specific entrances.
key_passages: The passers-by, thinking they are addressing a fellow Bostonian, don't even break their stride when they give their replies, which consist of a few words like 'First stop on the subway.'
This time the tapes show that people's answers are much longer and more rudimentary.
contestable_aspects: The representativeness of Harvard Square pedestrians.
The directness of the link between verbal directions and reading comprehension.

cases.name: SAT Score Decline 1972-1984
cases.description: A period where verbal SAT scores dropped significantly, particularly among high-performing students, which Hirsch uses to disprove that the decline was merely a result of broader test-taker demographics.
key_passages: out of a constant pool of about a million test takers each year, 56 percent more students scored above 600 in 1972 than did so in 1984.
contestable_aspects: The interpretation of SAT scores as a direct proxy for literacy/shared knowledge.
The assumption of a 'constant pool' of test takers.

cases.name: Los Angeles Teenager Focus Groups
cases.description: Observations conducted by Benjamin Stein with high school and college students in Los Angeles, revealing profound gaps in basic historical, geographical, and civic knowledge.
key_passages: I have not yet found one single student in Los Angeles, in either college or high school, who could tell me the years when World War II was fought.
Only two could even approximately identify Thomas Jefferson.
contestable_aspects: Representativeness of Los Angeles students for the whole nation.
The reliability of focus group anecdotes as empirical data.

cases.name: Richmond experiment
cases.description: A study conducted by Hirsch in Richmond, Virginia, involving 17- and 18-year-old subjects. The study revealed that these students lacked even the most basic historical knowledge, such as the identities of Civil War generals Grant and Lee, leading Hirsch to realize that reading skills are tied to specific background knowledge.
key_passages: In one experiment conducted in Richmond, Virginia, our seventeen- and eighteen-year-old subjects did not know who Grant and Lee were. Second, our results caused me to realize that we cannot treat reading and writing as empty skills, independent of specific knowledge.

cases.name: Hirsch's Father's Commodity Business
cases.description: A case study of business communication in the mid-20th century where the phrase 'There is a tide' from Julius Caesar effectively communicated a complex business strategy (act now or lose out) and its justifications in just four words.
key_passages: In my father's commodity business, the timing of sales and purchases was all-important, and he would sometimes write or say to his colleagues, 'There is a tide,' without further elaboration.
To say 'There is a tide' is better than saying 'Buy (or sell) now and you'll cover expenses for the whole year...'

cases.name: The US Political System
cases.description: The author discusses the US republic as a system founded on the principle that citizens are the masters, provided they have the literacy to deliberate and understand issues.
key_passages: Universal literacy is inseparable from democracy and is the canvas for Martin Luther King's picture as well as for Thomas Jefferson's.

cases.name: British Educational Satire (1066 and All That)
cases.description: Hirsch uses the British book '1066 and All That' as a natural experiment showing that even when specific historical dates and details become 'vague' in adult memory, the shared exposure to that information during schooling provides a stable, functional background for cultural communication and humor.
key_passages: It dealt with facts of British history that all educated Britons had been taught as children but remembered only dimly as adults.
Readers instantly recognized that the book was mistaken in its theory about what Ethelred-the-Unready was unready for, but, on the other hand, they couldn't say precisely what he was unready for.

cases.name: Indian and American Wedding Study
cases.description: Researchers paired readers in India and the US with similar backgrounds and gave them letters describing weddings. Though texts were equal in linguistic complexity, comprehension speed and accuracy depended entirely on whether the cultural context of the wedding matched the reader's nationality.
key_passages: The reading performances of the two groups — their speed and accuracy of comprehension — split along national lines. The Indians performed well in reading about the Indian wedding but poorly in reading about the American one, and the Americans did the opposite.

cases.name: The American High School (late 20th century)
cases.description: Described as a 'shopping mall' where diversity of choice is prioritized over a core curriculum, leading to students from the same school having no shared knowledge base.
key_passages: high schools offer courses of so many kinds that 'the word "curriculum" does not do justice to this astonishing variety.'
Those who graduate from the same school have often studied different subjects

cases.name: The Black Panther Newspaper
cases.description: A radical and revolutionary newspaper that, despite its political stance, adhered strictly to traditional linguistic conventions, including correct spelling and extensive use of traditional American historical and biblical allusions.
key_passages: Yet the Panther was highly conservative in its language and cultural assumptions, as it had to be in order to communicate effectively.
I have not found a single misspelled word in the many pages of radical sentiment I have examined in that newspaper.

cases.name: Hirsch's Southern Upbringing
cases.description: Hirsch describes how his own traditional education in a conservative Southern community provided the literacy tools to read 'An American Dilemma' and reject his family's conservative views.
key_passages: As a child of eleven, I turned against the conservative views of my family and the Southern community in which I grew up, precisely because I had been given a traditional education

cases.name: Virginia School Curriculum Priority
cases.description: A hypothetical scenario in Virginia where schools must choose between teaching a local hero (Jeb Stuart) and a national figure (Abraham Lincoln). Hirsch argues for Lincoln based on the requirements of national literacy.
key_passages: if Virginians did have to decide between Stuart and Lincoln they ought to favor the man from Illinois over the one from Virginia.

cases.name: The Virginia Curriculum Choice
cases.description: Hirsch uses the hypothetical choice between teaching Virginians about Jeb Stuart (local hero) vs. Abraham Lincoln (national figure) to argue for the priority of national cultural literacy over local knowledge.
key_passages: Therefore, if Virginians did have to decide between Stuart and Lincoln they ought to favor the man from Illinois over the one from Virginia. All literate Americans know traditional information about Abraham Lincoln but relatively few know about Jeb Stuart.

cases.name: US Early Reading Performance
cases.description: The author contrasts US performance in early grades (high international standing in mechanical decoding) with later grades (lower standing in mature reading tasks) to demonstrate the failure to teach background knowledge.
key_passages: Before grade three, when reading skills are more mechanical than interpretive, the United States stands in the top group of countries. Later... our comparative ranking drops.

cases.name: US Reading Instruction
cases.description: American schools successfully teach technical decoding but fail to provide the cultural knowledge required for mature reading, leading to a widening gap by the fourth grade.
key_passages: while American schools may do well in teaching elementary decoding skills, they do less well than schools of some other countries in teaching the background knowledge that pupils must possess to succeed at mature reading tasks.

cases.name: Cultural Literacy 1945 vs 1980s
cases.description: A comparison showing how specific items like 'DNA' and 'Brown decision' entered the common lexicon while others like 'Harold Ickes' faded, illustrating the mutability of the periphery.
key_passages: Today the term 'Brown decision' belongs to cultural literacy, but in 1945 there hadn't been any Brown decision. The name Harold Ickes was current in 1945 but no longer is.

cases.name: Human Relations Area Files
cases.description: A standard anthropological database used to demonstrate that all reported cultures use early memorization of traditions.
key_passages: According to the anthropological record, all cultures whose educational methods have been reported in the Human Relations Area Files... have used early memorization to carry on their traditions.

thinkers.name: Jeanne S. Chall
thinkers.key_idea: The necessity of 'world knowledge' for literacy and the 'great debate' over methods of reading instruction.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch adopts her concept of 'world knowledge' but rebrands it as 'cultural literacy' and uses her observation of the literacy crisis as a starting point for his argument.
key_passages: one of its major purposes is to break away entirely from what Jeanne S. Chall has called 'the great debate' about methods of reading instruction.
Professor Chall is one of several reading specialists who have observed that 'world knowledge' is essential to the development of reading and writing skills.

thinkers.name: Henry Bradley
thinkers.key_idea: Mass literacy has a stabilizing effect on oral speech.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Bradley's work to support the claim that standard written language fixes and standardizes spoken forms.
key_passages: The two classical discussions of the stabilizing effects of mass literacy on oral speech are Henry Bradley, The Making of English

thinkers.name: John B. Carroll
thinkers.key_idea: The verbal SAT is primarily a measure of 'advanced vocabulary knowledge.'
thinkers.source_work: Psychometric Approaches to the Study of Language Abilities (1979)
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Carroll's expertise to validate the verbal SAT as a legitimate proxy for measuring sophisticated literacy levels.
key_passages: According to John B. Carroll, a distinguished psychometrician, the verbal SAT is essentially a test of 'advanced vocabulary knowledge,' which makes it a fairly sensitive instrument for measuring levels of literacy.

thinkers.name: Benjamin J. Stein
thinkers.key_idea: The extreme lack of basic historical and geographic knowledge among contemporary American youth.
thinkers.source_work: The Cheerful Ignorance of the Young in L.A. (1983)
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch quotes Stein extensively to provide 'evocative' evidence of the specific information gaps that constitute a decline in cultural literacy.
key_passages: The following, by Benjamin J. Stein, is an excerpt from one of the most evocative recent accounts of youthful ignorance.

thinkers.name: Orlando Patterson
thinkers.key_idea: Cultural literacy is a requirement of industrialized civilization and is essential for minorities to gain social and economic power.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Patterson to argue that cultural literacy is not an elitist or ethnocentric concern but a matter of social justice and structural necessity for modern life.
key_passages: He seconded my argument that shared information is a necessary background to true literacy. Then he extended and deepened the ideas I had presented.
blacks will be condemned in perpetuity to oversimplified, low-level tasks... unless they also acquire literacy in this wider cultural sense.

thinkers.name: Mina Shaughnessy
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch mentions Shaughnessy to frame his argument within the context of helping the disadvantaged, though he suggests her focus on basic literacy may need to be expanded to cultural literacy.
key_passages: Mina Shaughnessy was a great English teacher who devoted her professional life to helping disadvantaged students become literate.

thinkers.name: Thomas Jefferson
thinkers.key_idea: Democratic government relies on citizens receiving and being capable of reading newspapers.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch aligns his argument for cultural literacy with Jefferson's foundational vision of a literate democracy.
key_passages: But I should mean that every man should receive those papers and be capable of reading them.

thinkers.name: Martin Luther King, Jr.
thinkers.key_idea: A society where individuals are judged by merit and character rather than race or class.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch argues that King's dream of equality depends on 'mature literacy' to be realized in a modern society.
key_passages: Universal literacy is inseparable from democracy and is the canvas for Martin Luther King's picture as well as for Thomas Jefferson's.

thinkers.name: Hilary Putnam
thinkers.key_idea: The division of linguistic labor and the role of stereotypic knowledge in word acquisition.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Putnam's philosophical insights to provide a theoretical foundation for the necessity of 'vague' and 'superficial' cultural knowledge in communication.
key_passages: one of the sharpest critical thinkers of our day, Dr. Hilary Putnam, a Harvard philosopher, has provided us with a profound insight into the importance of vague knowledge in verbal communication.

thinkers.name: Richard C. Anderson
thinkers.key_idea: Schema theory and the cross-cultural dependence of reading comprehension.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Anderson's empirical research at the University of Illinois to provide scientific proof that literacy is tied to national cultural knowledge.
key_passages: The national character of the knowledge needed in reading and writing was strikingly revealed in an experiment conducted by Richard C. Anderson

thinkers.name: Jean-Jacques Rousseau
thinkers.key_idea: The origin of content-neutral educational ideas that prioritize natural development over specific knowledge.
thinkers.engagement: The author identifies Rousseau as one of the two primary sources of the content-neutral ideology dominating and harming American schools.
key_passages: Our elementary schools are not only dominated by the content-neutral ideas of Rousseau and Dewey

thinkers.name: John Dewey
thinkers.key_idea: Promoted content-neutral, process-oriented education that neglected specific shared information.
thinkers.engagement: The author links Dewey to the fragmentation of the school curriculum and the decline of teaching cultural literacy.
key_passages: Our elementary schools are not only dominated by the content-neutral ideas of Rousseau and Dewey

thinkers.name: Herbert Walberg
thinkers.key_idea: Schools are responsible for excessive student TV watching because they fail to assign sufficient homework.
thinkers.engagement: The author uses Walberg's findings to argue that the literacy crisis is a failure of school policy rather than an inevitable result of technology.
key_passages: as Herbert Walberg points out, the schools themselves must be held partly responsible for excessive television watching, because they have not firmly insisted that students complete significant amounts of homework

thinkers.name: Gunnar Myrdal
thinkers.key_idea: An American Dilemma: The Negro Problem and Modern Democracy, a critique of American racial policy.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Myrdal's book as the specific text that radicalized him against his conservative upbringing, using it to show that literacy leads to critical thinking.
key_passages: I was therefore literate enough to read Gunnar MyrdaPs An American Dilemma, an epoch-making book in my life.

thinkers.name: Oscar Wilde
thinkers.key_idea: Irony and the myth-making nature of national heroes, specifically attacking the George Washington cherry-tree myth.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Wilde to show that one can be culturally literate (knowing the Washington myth) while simultaneously being critical, ironic, or even hostile toward the values the myth purports to teach.
key_passages: Oscar Wilde in 'The Decay of Lying' used it ironically... For us no less than for Wilde, the values affirmed in traditional literate culture can serve a whole spectrum of value attitudes.

thinkers.name: Lawrence A. Cremin
thinkers.key_idea: The history and transformation of American schools through Progressivism.
thinkers.source_work: The Transformation of the American School: Progressivism in American Education, 1876-1957
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Cremin to provide historical context for the 'natural development' theories he is critiquing.
key_passages: L. A. Cremin, The Transformation of the American School: Progressivism in American Education, 1876-1957

thinkers.name: Richard Feynman
thinkers.key_idea: Ability to communicate complex expertise to a wide audience of educated people.
thinkers.engagement: Cited as an 'admirable writer' who proves that literate discourse can bridge the gap between specialized expertise and the general public.
key_passages: That this ideal can be achieved is proved by such admirable writers as... Richard Feynman, who are able to communicate their complex expertise to a wide audience of educated people.

actors.name: Jeanne S. Chall
actors.context: Reading specialist and professor who identified the importance of world knowledge in literacy.

actors.name: Linguists
actors.context: Used the term 'standard written English' to describe the stable forms of the language.

actors.name: American citizens
actors.context: One third are cited as being illiterate; the remaining two-thirds have an average level that is too low.

actors.name: National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP)
actors.context: Congressional mandated nationwide measurement of student achievement used to identify the decline in reading skills.

actors.name: The College Board
actors.context: Provided the full statistics in 1984 that revealed the decline in SAT scores extended to top-tier students.

actors.name: The College Board
actors.context: Source of statistical data regarding the decline of SAT scores.

actors.name: CBS and Exxon
actors.context: Large corporations whose executives expressed alarm over declining communication skills in employees.

actors.name: American Academy of Arts and Sciences
actors.context: Recipient of a grant to analyze the causes of business communication decline.

actors.name: NAEP
actors.context: The National Assessment of Educational Progress, cited for providing statistical data on the decline of civic knowledge.

actors.name: Foundations of Literacy project
actors.context: A project commissioned to NAEP to probe literary and historical knowledge of 17-year-olds.

actors.name: John Agresto
actors.context: Acting Chairman of the NEH who released the preliminary findings on youthful ignorance in 1985.

actors.name: Orlando Patterson
actors.context: Harvard historian and sociologist supporting the link between background knowledge and power.

actors.name: Mina Shaughnessy
actors.context: English teacher known for work with disadvantaged students, used to contextualize the conference where Patterson spoke.

actors.name: Orlando Patterson
actors.context: Sociologist at Harvard who argues for the necessity of wider cultural literacy for minorities.

actors.name: Martin Luther King, Jr.
actors.context: Civil rights leader whose vision of equality Hirsch links to literacy.

actors.name: Thomas Jefferson
actors.context: Founding Father whose democratic principles require a literate citizenry.

actors.name: WASP (White Anglo-Saxon Protestant)
actors.context: The group often associated with 'wider culture,' though Hirsch/Patterson argue the culture does not belong to them.

actors.name: John R. Block
actors.context: U.S. Agriculture Secretary mentioned in a Washington Post article used as an example of assumed knowledge.

actors.name: USDA
actors.context: The United States Department of Agriculture, mentioned as an acronym readers are expected to know.

actors.name: Hilary Putnam
actors.context: Harvard philosopher used to validate the necessity of superficial knowledge.

actors.name: Melvin Durslag
actors.context: Writer for TV Guide whose article on baseball is used as an example of how authors provide specific details while assuming general context.

actors.name: Center for the Study of Reading at the University of Illinois
actors.context: Conducted the pivotal study on Indian and American wedding letters.

actors.name: American Schools
actors.context: The entity tasked with the 'primary and fundamental' responsibility of acculturation.

actors.name: American Elementary Schools
actors.context: Characterized as failing in their primary responsibility due to ideological domination and curriculum fragmentation.

actors.name: National literate culture
actors.context: The specific community level that Hirsch argues schools must prioritize over tribal or world cultures.

actors.name: School Administrators
actors.context: Accused of using educational formalism to stay neutral and avoid ideological conflict at the expense of shared knowledge.

actors.name: Liberal/Conservative ideological parties
actors.context: The groups whose conflicting demands on curriculum drove schools toward a formalistic, quantitative approach.

actors.name: The Black Panther Party
actors.context: Used as an example of radical political actors who utilized traditional cultural literacy for effective communication.

actors.name: Middle-class children
actors.context: Cited as the group that naturally acquires mainstream culture through daily social interaction.

actors.name: The Black Panthers
actors.context: Cited as a group that used traditional cultural symbols to effectively communicate radical messages.

actors.name: Progressive Educators
actors.context: Those who withhold traditional culture from students in the name of progressive ideas, which Hirsch claims is actually unprogressive.

actors.name: The Disadvantaged
actors.context: The group most harmed by the failure of schools to explicitly teach cultural literacy.

actors.name: United States Schools
actors.context: Characterized as successful at teaching decoding but failing at teaching background knowledge.

actors.name: Disadvantaged children
actors.context: The group most negatively affected by the lack of cultural content in early schooling.

actors.name: American Schools
actors.context: Characterized as doing well with decoding but poorly with background knowledge.

actors.name: Araucanian Indians
actors.context: Used as an example of a culture that uses song memorization for education in Chile.

actors.name: !Kung bushmen
actors.context: Used as an example of a culture where children learn the history of objects through listening to adult discussion.

actors.name: Technicians
actors.context: Specialists with 'arcane specialties' who risk being unable to communicate with the democratic public if shared literacy is not maintained.

actors.name: American Schools
actors.context: The entity responsible for ensuring the command of shared background knowledge to maintain a just society.

objections_raised.objection: Literacy is primarily a general, transferable skill of decoding and processing text.
objections_raised.response: Literacy requires large amounts of specific, shared information (background knowledge) to be effective.

objections_raised.objection: Standard written English is a dialect that is 'intrinsically superior' to other dialects.
objections_raised.response: It has no intrinsic superiority; its value lies in its stability and its role as a national instrument for communication over time and space.

objections_raised.objection: The fall in average verbal SAT scores is caused by the increase in the number of disadvantaged students taking the test, rather than a general decline in education.
objections_raised.response: Full statistics released in 1984 show that the number of high-scoring, talented students is also declining out of a constant pool of test-takers.

objections_raised.objection: Students are not actually ignorant; they know a great deal of things.
objections_raised.response: They do share a lot of knowledge, but it is ephemeral and generation-specific, lacking the intergenerational breadth required for national literacy.

objections_raised.objection: Cultural literacy is just an artifact of white Anglo-Saxon Protestant culture.
objections_raised.response: Patterson argues that cultural literacy is now driven by the functional imperatives of industrial civilization, not ethnic identity.

objections_raised.objection: The 'wider culture' is a static WASP culture used for domination.
objections_raised.response: The culture is actually a changing, open 'dialectic' to which all groups contribute and are transformed by.

objections_raised.objection: The knowledge required for cultural literacy is too vast, detailed, and precise for all citizens to master.
